hype.text: England doing what England does best and boring us all into a £20 refund — the only scenario where Ethan actually breaks even without selling a kidney to keep that motor on the road. Watching Argentina is basically monitoring Ethan's credit score live: one Messi masterclass and he might afford this month's PCP payment; one flop and the bailiffs get a new pin on the map. Come on Argentina, the whole group needs this pot paid out before Ethan's finance company sends a recovery truck as his celebration parade.

Will's got Spain, which is handy because his PhD thesis on "structural integrity of playground equipment" is the only thing keeping that defence upright after he threw his back out saving toddlers in Prague. Ethan's Argentina side mirrors the man perfectly: all flashy attack, zero engine, and financed on a balloon payment he'll be explaining to the bailiffs until 2035. One's carrying a nation on a knackered spine, the other's carrying negative equity on a German motor — perfect sweepstake material.

Bell's spent all winter hibernating indoors like a bored house spider, so it's only right his France side turn up for a glorified kickabout against an England team still traumatised by losing a semi and playing for the wooden spoon. The £20 refund is dead and buried, mate — much like Bell's social life — but at least he can console himself identifying the creepy-crawlies on the pitch while the Three Lions pretend bronze means something.

Will has won the World Cup, and he did it the way he does everything: quietly, methodically, and without watching. It was that kind of tournament — debutants Cape Verde held Spain 0-0 in the group opener (the eventual champions, held by a country of half a million — Will found out weeks later), Paraguay knocked out Germany on penalties despite completing 161 passes to Germany's 719, and the White House leaned on Infantino to overturn a US red card, which everyone agreed was fine and normal. Honour where it's due before the roasting: Todd's Norway knocked out Brazil — Brazil! — in the last sixteen before going down 2-1 to England, so his £20 did more for this country than most governments. Darley's Belgium humiliated the host nation 4-1 before Spain ended them 2-1: £20 gone, in London prices, but at least it annoyed America first. Dom's Morocco went quietly, 2-0 to France, taken well by a man who only recently decided he likes football. Perez's Switzerland were dismantled 3-1 by Argentina — who, it should be noted, had already needed to come back from 2-0 down against Egypt with a Messi penalty saved. Ethan's team spent the whole tournament nearly losing, which tracks. Then Atlanta. England 1-0 up in the semi, the whole group at peace with donating £20 to football coming home — and Argentina produced the full repertoire: fifteen fouls to our eleven, three yellows to one, Fernández in the back of Anderson, Romero chirping, and Lautaro's 92nd-minute winner off a Messi cross half the country insists came from a foul. Robbed at the death by the dark arts, defended all week by Ethan. England's consolation was the game of the tournament — 6-4 over Bell's France for the bronze, ten goals Bell couldn't name a scorer from. And then, justice in New Jersey: Fernández launched Cubarsí into orbit in stoppage time and got the red he'd owed us since Atlanta; Ethan's keeper made a World Cup final record ten saves — one man doing everything while those around him contribute nothing, a dynamic Ethan understands — and 39 seconds into the second half of extra time, Ferran Torres finished ten-man Argentina off. Messi managed fifteen touches. Ethan managed one goal fewer than £140. The car finance survives another quarter. So the pot goes to Will — PhD, Prague slide hero, couldn't pick Torres out of a line-up — off the back of a team that conceded once all tournament. It's not coming home. It's going to Warwick, which is nowhere near as good. Same again in 2030.
